common_name: Brazil Nut Tree (Para Nut, Castanheiro do Para)

planting.note: Plant container-grown seedlings in spring; brazil nut trees require deep, fertile, well-drained tropical soil and high humidity — they are strictly zone 11-12 trees that rarely fruit outside their native Amazonian habitat; grown in the US primarily in south Florida and Hawaii as specimen trees.

directions: Plant in full sun in deep, well-drained, fertile, moist to wet tropical soil; Brazil nut (Bertholletia excelsa) is one of the largest trees of the Amazon rainforest, reaching 150 feet tall, and requires 10–20 years from seed to first fruiting in optimal conditions. The pods (actually large, woody capsules weighing up to 5 lbs) each contain 8–24 seeds (the 'nuts') and fall from height, posing a safety hazard — harvest sites require protective gear. The Brazil nut ecosystem depends entirely on the orchid bee (Eulaema sp.) for pollination and the agouti (a large rodent) for seed dispersal, making it nearly impossible to grow commercially outside its native ecosystem. Trees in south Florida and Hawaii produce limited fruit but are grown primarily as impressive specimen trees; the heavy, dense, rot-resistant wood is also extremely valuable.

planting.note: Plant grafted or container-grown specimens in spring in frost-free conditions; pili nut requires deep, fertile, well-drained soil in the humid tropics; best suited to zone 10b-12 in the United States, primarily south Florida and Hawaii.

directions: Plant in full sun in deep, fertile, well-drained tropical soil; pili nut (Canarium ovatum) is the premier nut crop of the Philippines, producing large, elongated, olive-shaped brown nuts with an extremely rich, buttery, almond-cashew flavor and high oil content (70–80% fat, similar to macadamia) that many food experts rank among the most delicious nuts in the world. The tree is a close relative of elemi resin trees and produces fragrant, semi-translucent resin used in varnishes. Nuts are eaten raw, roasted, candied, or pressed for cooking oil; the pulp surrounding the nut shell is also edible, used in Filipino cuisine for cooking and flavoring. Increasingly cultivated in Hawaii and south Florida as a high-value specialty nut crop by tropical fruit enthusiasts.

planting.note: Plant seedlings or cuttings in full sun in well-drained soil; candlenut is the state tree of Hawaii (kukui) and can be grown throughout zone 10–12; it tolerates a wide range of soils and is relatively fast-growing. Note: raw nuts are toxic — they must be roasted or cooked before eating.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile to moderately fertile soil in frost-free conditions; candlenut (Aleurites moluccanus, kukui in Hawaiian) is an extremely versatile tree native to tropical Asia and the Pacific Islands, widely cultivated for its large, oily nuts that are a critical ingredient in Indonesian (kemiri), Malaysian, and Hawaiian cuisine — the roasted kernels are ground into pastes, sauces, and curries. Caution: raw candlenuts are toxic and mildly purgative due to saponins; always roast or cook before eating. The nuts have the highest oil content of any edible nut and burn like candles, giving the tree its common name; the oil was traditionally used for lighting, wood finishing, and skin care. The Hawaii state tree (kukui), with distinctive silvery-green foliage visible from a distance.

planting.note: Plant grafted named varieties in spring in deep, moist, fertile, well-drained soil; hicans require a cross-pollinator from either pecan or shagbark hickory for reliable crop; named hican varieties include 'McCallister', 'Pleas', and 'Burton'.

directions: Plant grafted named-variety trees in deep, moist, fertile, well-drained bottomland soil in full sun; hicans are natural and intentionally bred hybrids between pecan (Carya illinoinensis) and various hickory species (most commonly shagbark or bitternut), combining traits of both parents — the nuts are usually larger and easier to crack than hickory but with a richer, more complex flavor than straight pecan, with less oil and a firmer, drier kernel. Trees are typically very vigorous and disease-resistant, combining pecan's productivity tendency with hickory's cold hardiness and adaptability. Suitable for zones 5–9 where pecan may be too tender or unreliable; an excellent choice for nut tree diversification in humid eastern forests. Requires cross-pollination from pecan or a compatible hickory nearby.

planting.note: Plant balled-and-burlapped or bare-root trees in spring; mockernut hickory has a deep taproot and should be planted young — larger transplants rarely establish successfully; requires deep, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant young container or bare-root trees in full sun in deep, well-drained, moderately fertile to fertile soil in spring; mockernut hickory is the most common hickory in the eastern United States and among the most beautiful, with dramatic large compound leaves that turn brilliant golden-yellow in fall. The nuts are enclosed in a very thick husk (giving the 'mockernut' name — the husk 'mocks' the nut inside), and the kernel is small but has a rich, full, classic hickory flavor. A critical wildlife tree, providing extremely high-value mast for deer, turkey, squirrels, and many other species; the very dense, strong, flexible wood is commercially valuable for tool handles and sporting goods. An outstanding native shade tree for large properties that also provides excellent wildlife food.
